analogy: Think of "Chemical Reactions and Equations" through the chapter's own opening images: Chemical Reactions and Equations 1 CHAPTER Consider the following situations of daily life and think what happens when – n milk is left at room temperature during summers. n an iron tawa/pan/nail is left exposed to humid atmosphere. n grapes get fermented. n food is... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Chemical Reactions and Equations is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that chemical Reactions and Equations 1 CHAPTER Consider the following situations of daily life and think what happens when – n milk is left at room temperature during summers. n an iron tawa/pan/nail is left exposed to humid atmosphere. n grapes get fermented. n food is cooked. n food gets digested in our body. n we respire.

The text explains that in all the above situations, the nature and the identity of the initial substance have somewhat changed.

The text explains that we have already learnt about physical and chemical changes of matter in our previous classes.

The text explains that whenever a chemical change occurs, we can say that a chemical reaction has taken place.

The text explains that you may perhaps be wondering as to what is actually meant by a chemical reaction.

The text explains that how do we come to know that a chemical reaction has taken place?

The text explains that let us perform some activities to find the answer to these questions.

The text explains that figure 1.1 Burning of a magnesium ribbon in air and collection of magnesium oxide in a watch-glass Activity 1.1 Activity 1.1 Activity 1.1 Activity 1.1 Activity 1.1 CAUTION: This Activity needs the teacher’s assistance.

The text explains that it would be better if students wear suitable eyeglasses. n Clean a magnesium ribbon about 3-4 cm long by rubbing it with sandpaper. n Hold it with a pair of tongs.

The text explains that burn it using a spirit lamp or burner and collect the ash so formed in a watch-glass as shown in Fig.

The text explains that burn the magnesium ribbon keeping it away as far as possible from your eyes. n What do you observe? “Facts are not science — as the dictionary is not literature.” Martin H.

The text explains that science 2 Activity 1.2 Activity 1.2 Activity 1.2 Activity 1.2 Activity 1.2 Figure 1.2 Figure 1.2 Figure 1.2 Figure 1.2 Figure 1.2 Formation of hydrogen gas by the action of dilute sulphuric acid on zinc From the above three activities, we can say that any of the following observations helps us to determine whether a chemical reaction has taken place – n change in state n change in colour n evolution of a gas n change in temperature.

The text explains that as we observe the changes around us, we can see that there is a large variety of chemical reactions taking place around us.

The text explains that we will study about the various types of chemical reactions and their symbolic representation in this Chapter.

The text explains that cAUTION: Handle the acid with care. n Do you observe anything happening around the zinc granules? n Touch the conical flask or test tube.

The text explains that is there any change in its temperature? n Take lead nitrate solution in a test tube. n Add potassium iodide solution to this. n What do you observe?

The text explains that the arrowhead points towards the products, and shows the direction of the reaction.

The text explains that sometimes the reaction conditions, such as temperature, pressure, catalyst, etc., for the reaction are indicated above and/or below the arrow in the equation.

The text explains that 1.3. n Do you feel any change in temperature?

The text explains that science 16 Group Activity Perform the following activity. n Take four beakers and label them as A, B, C and D. n Put 25 mL of water in A, B and C beakers and copper sulphate solution in beaker D. n Measure and record the temperature of each liquid contained in the beakers above. n Add two spatulas of potassium sulphate, ammonium nitrate, anhydrous copper sulphate and fine iron fillings to beakers A, B, C and D respectively and stir. n Finally measure and record the temperature of each of the mixture above.

The text explains that find out which reactions are exothermic and which ones are endothermic in nature.

The text explains that write one equation each for decomposition reactions where energy is supplied in the form of heat, light or electricity.

The text explains that what is the difference between displacement and double displacement reactions?

The text explains that in the refining of silver, the recovery of silver from silver nitrate solution involved displacement by copper metal.

The text explains that what do you mean by a precipitation reaction?

The text explains that explain the following in terms of gain or loss of oxygen with two examples each.

The text explains that a shiny brown coloured element ‘X’ on heating in air becomes black in colour.

The text explains that name the element ‘X’ and the black coloured compound formed.

The text explains that oil and fat containing food items are flushed with nitrogen.

The text explains that explain the following terms with one example each.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 1: Chemical Reactions and Equations

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 CHAPTER and 1.1.1 Writing a Chemical Equation as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Chemical Reactions and Equations; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Chemical Reactions and Equations through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 CHAPTER and 1.1.1 Writing a Chemical Equation to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
